article.title: Ayar Labs Raises $500M Series E to Scale Optical Interconnects for AI Data Centers
article.summary: Optical chip startup Ayar Labs closes $500M Series E at $3.75B valuation, backed by NVIDIA, AMD, and MediaTek, to replace copper interconnects with light-based data transmission in AI infrastructure.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Ayar Labs, a startup developing optical interconnect technology for data centers, has closed a $500 million Series E funding round at a $3.75 billion valuation, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/03/co-packaged-optics-startup-ayar-labs-raises-500m-round-backed-nvidia-amd/). The round was led by Neuberger Berman, with participation from chip giants NVIDIA, AMD, and MediaTek as strategic investors.

The funding brings Ayar Labs' total capital raised to $870 million and positions the company to scale production of its co-packaged optics hardware, which transmits data between server chips using light rather than traditional copper wiring.

## The Copper Bottleneck

As AI training clusters grow to tens of thousands of GPUs, the copper interconnects that link processors together have become a significant performance constraint. Signal degradation, energy losses, and latency all worsen at the higher speeds demanded by modern AI workloads. Forward error correction technology used in copper-based systems can introduce latency penalties of approximately 100 milliseconds, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/03/co-packaged-optics-startup-ayar-labs-raises-500m-round-backed-nvidia-amd/).

Ayar Labs CEO Mark Wade has described the challenge in direct terms, stating that the company is "solving one of the biggest hardware issues that's causing bottlenecks in AI."

## How the Technology Works

Ayar Labs' approach centers on two complementary chips. The first, called SuperNova, is a light-emitting chip roughly the size of a dime that generates laser beams used for data transmission. The second, called TeraPHY, is an optical processing chip containing millions of transistors and miniaturized optical devices, including microring resonators, according to [SiliconANGLE](https://siliconangle.com/2026/03/03/co-packaged-optics-startup-ayar-labs-raises-500m-round-backed-nvidia-amd/).

The TeraPHY chip can process up to 8 terabits of data per second and supports the UCIe industry standard, enabling direct integration with GPUs. The company claims its optical interconnects deliver 4 to 20 times greater computing throughput per watt compared to traditional copper connections.

## Strategic Backing

The investor roster underscores how central the interconnect problem has become for the semiconductor industry. NVIDIA and AMD, the two dominant suppliers of AI accelerators, both participated as strategic investors, alongside chip design firm MediaTek and semiconductor services company Alchip Technologies, according to [Ayar Labs' announcement](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260303608615/en/Ayar-Labs-Closes-$500M-Series-E-Accelerates-Volume-Production-of-Co-Packaged-Optics).

Financial investors in the round included ARK Invest, Insight Partners, Qatar Investment Authority, Sequoia Global Equities, and 1789 Capital, according to [Ayar Labs' announcement](https://www.businesswire.com/news/home/20260303608615/en/Ayar-Labs-Closes-$500M-Series-E-Accelerates-Volume-Production-of-Co-Packaged-Optics).

## Use of Funds

Ayar Labs plans to use the capital to scale high-volume production of its co-packaged optics components, expand testing capacity, and grow its international operations. The company recently opened an office in Hsinchu, Taiwan, positioning it closer to the Asia-Pacific semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem.

## Market Context

The raise arrives amid a wave of large funding rounds in AI infrastructure. With global data center construction accelerating and AI model sizes continuing to grow, the physical layer of computing infrastructure has attracted increasing investor attention. Optical interconnects represent one of several approaches to overcoming the bandwidth and power efficiency limitations that constrain current AI hardware deployments.
